title: Public Works Committee
overview: The Village of Weston Public Works Committee approved several contracts and agreements including recycled asphalt crushing to King Gravel for $126,900, a transportation funding resolution, and construction services for upcoming road projects. The committee also discussed enforcement of sanitary sewer discharge violations, directing staff to create language for $100/day fines for violations.

agenda.item: Acknowledge April 2026 Water and Sewer Permits
Water and Sewer Connection Agreement for Radkey Avenue

discussions.body: Michael reported the village received a Local Road Improvement Grant for Birch Street reconstruction between Schofield Avenue and Community Center Drive, originally planned for 2029 but likely moving to 2028. The grant covers over 50% of the estimated $1.5 million project. Michael noted the village has received over $9 million in grant funding over the past 4-5 years. Discussion also covered Weston Avenue repairs by Integrity Grading and Excavating for settlement issues, and upcoming manhole repairs on Schofield Avenue by HOS Construction.

Staff reported road limits were lifted on April 29th. Spring activities included mailbox repairs, sign repairs, curb line sweeping, and hot mix patching. Storm damage cleanup was done on East Nick. A culvert failure on Jones Street required installation of a third 18-inch culvert. Equipment maintenance was detailed including changeover from winter to summer operations. Staff reported an additional 1,500 tons of salt ordered for next season.

Josh reported significant clear water infiltration into the sanitary system, with April showing 37 million gallons pumped into distribution but 53-54 million gallons treated at the wastewater facility - approximately 15 million gallons of clear water. Discussion focused on sump pumps illegally connected to sanitary sewers. An INI study using In-Pipe Technologies will help identify problem areas. The committee discussed enforcement strategies for violations.

Michael presented the annual report showing operating revenues of approximately $3 million with net operating income of $482,911, consistent with 2024's $490,000. Water loss was 6%. The utility value increased to nearly $34 million with $6.7 million in infrastructure placed in service. The utility has approximately 6,100 customers. Approved unanimously to acknowledge.

Michael explained this League of Wisconsin Municipalities sample resolution supports improved state transportation funding for local operations. The resolution notes Wisconsin has lower automobile ownership costs than neighboring states. The committee voted to recommend village board adoption. Approved unanimously.

Michael reported King Gravel submitted the low bid at $4.23/ton for crushing approximately 30,000 tons of recycled material, totaling $126,900. Analysis showed the village saves approximately $11/cubic yard compared to City of Wausau costs, representing about $130,000 savings on 2026 projects. Motion to award contract to King Gravel passed unanimously.

The committee discussed enforcement of sewer discharge violations, particularly sump pumps illegally connected to sanitary sewers. Current code allows fines of $100-$1,000 per day. The committee directed staff to create enforcement language with fines at $100/day for violations, distinguishing between unknowing and willful violations. Approved unanimously.

discussions.item: Water and Sewer Connection Agreement for Radkey Avenue
discussions.body: Michael presented an agreement allowing a duplex at 2230 Radkey Avenue to temporarily connect via long lateral service until Radkey Avenue reconstruction, at which point they would pay assessment for main extension. The committee added language specifying reconstruction no later than 2029. Motion passed unanimously.

Michael presented an intergovernmental agreement with Village of Rothschild for Military Road construction. Weston's share for utility work is $94,860 (sanitary sewer $6,156; water main $88,704). Steam Construction is the contractor. Motion to recommend board approval passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Construction Services for Alderson/Jelinek and Bladel Projects
discussions.body: Michael recommended MSA for Alderson/Jelinek roundabout project construction services at $37,315, and Clark Dietz for Bladel Avenue project at $73,730 due to having experienced full-time staff rather than interns. Both recommendations approved unanimously.

actionItems: Award 2026 recycled asphalt crushing contract to King Gravel at $4.23/ton (estimated $126,900)
Recommend village board adopt Resolution 2026-014 supporting state transportation funding
Direct staff to create enforcement language for sanitary sewer discharge violations at $100/day fines
Approve temporary water/sewer connection agreement for 2230 Radkey Avenue with reconstruction deadline of 2029
Recommend board approval of Military Road construction agreement with Rothschild for $94,860
Award construction services to MSA for Alderson/Jelinek project and Clark Dietz for Bladel project
Schedule special assessment meeting for Bladel Avenue on May 28, 2026 at 6:00 PM
Staff to provide cost analysis of clear water treatment costs at next meeting
Consider street lighting for Schofield Avenue from Birch Street to Camp Phillips Road in 2030 project

title: Tourism Commission
overview: The Village of Weston Tourism Commission held its reorganization meeting, electing new officers and approving a $5,000 grant for the Taste and Glow Balloonfest. The commission noted that a competing hot air balloon event in Marshfield has been canceled, potentially boosting attendance at the Weston event.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Approval of minutes from April 21st meeting
New business - Grant request for Taste and Glow Balloonfest
Remarks from staff and commission members

discussions.body: Trustee Erling was nominated and elected as Chair unanimously. Cushing was nominated and elected as Vice Chair with Jackson providing the second. Renee was nominated and elected as Secretary. All elections passed unanimously with no opposition.
Minutes from the April 21st meeting were approved unanimously. The motion was seconded by Renee.

discussions.item: Grant Request - Taste and Glow Balloonfest
discussions.body: Brandon Jill of 3308 Green Pastures Lane presented on behalf of the Taste and Glow Balloonfest, reporting approximately 11,000-12,000 attendees last year. He announced that the competing Marshfield hot air balloon event has been canceled due to permit issues, making theirs the sole event in the area. Commission members questioned room tracking, noting the application showed 20 fewer rooms than last year. The commission approved a $5,000 grant matching last year's amount, with a request that organizers provide better hotel room data in their post-event report. Motion passed unanimously with Jackson seconding.

Renee reported on attending the Alice in Dairyland dinner at Red Rock Orchard in Edgar, describing how candidates from across Wisconsin toured Marathon County locations including Mullen's Cheese. She noted this was the first time Marathon County hosted the event since the 1970s. The winner, Anastasia, sat at their table and the candidates expressed appreciation for the Marathon County community.

actionItems: Approved $5,000 grant to Taste and Glow Balloonfest
Taste and Glow organizers to provide hotel room usage data in post-event report
Next meeting scheduled for July 20th at 4:00 PM (tentative)

title: Plan Commission
overview: The Village of Weston Plan Commission approved site plans for the new Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive, and adopted a new salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection areas. The commission also scheduled the first steering committee meeting for the comprehensive plan update for June 1st.

agenda.item: Call to order and roll call
Site plan review for Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive
Creation of salt storage shed inspection program for Wellhead Protection District
April 2026 staff approved certified survey map and site plan
Planning and development department monthly project update report

discussions.item: Site plan review - Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility
discussions.body: Staff presented the site plan for the new police department and municipal court at 3600 Community Center Drive. The building was rotated and repositioned north to accommodate a 36-inch gas pipeline running under the parking lot and to reduce fill costs. Chief Jeremy explained the building will accommodate 50-60 officers for future growth beyond current 40 officers and 4 clerical staff. Discussion addressed an alternate storage shed placement, with commissioners suggesting moving it further north to allow for future building expansion. The landscaping plan meets current relaxed code requirements. A motion to recommend approval to the Board of Trustees with staff conditions 2-5 passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Salt storage shed inspection program for Wellhead Protection District
discussions.body: Staff proposed a new inspection program with two annual inspections per facility - a warm weather inspection focused on structural integrity and drainage, and a winter operational inspection focused on material handling and containment. A standardized inspection worksheet similar to DOT standards was developed. Staff noted one existing facility near the municipal well on Sternberg has never gone through the wellhead protection conditional use process and will be contacted about compliance. The motion to approve the program with reporting back to both Plan Commission and Public Works Committee passed unanimously.
Motion to approve as presented was made and seconded. Approved unanimously without discussion.

discussions.item: April 2026 staff approved certified survey map and site plan
discussions.body: Approved unanimously with minimal discussion.
Approved unanimously with minimal discussion.

discussions.item: Planning and development department monthly update
discussions.body: Staff announced the first joint Plan Commission and Village Board steering committee meeting for the comprehensive plan is scheduled for Monday, June 1st at 5:00 PM. Staff will send email notifications and calendar invites.

discussions.item: Future agenda items and commissioner remarks
discussions.body: Electric vehicle charging stations will be added as a future agenda item, likely for fall. A commissioner inquired about potential new TIF districts for residential/workforce housing. Staff mentioned Ehlers has prepared a presentation on residential TIF qualifications that could be shared with the commission, possibly as part of the comprehensive plan process.

publicComment: Jim Pensel of 5002 Arrow Street, Weston, spoke via online participation. He urged the commission to exercise common sense regarding landscaping requirements for the police department project to avoid excessive taxpayer costs, referencing relaxed standards used for the Greenheck Turner building. He also requested that the salt shed inspection program include annual reporting on site visits and findings.
actionItems: Site plan for Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive recommended for approval to Board of Trustees with staff conditions
Salt storage shed inspection program adopted for wellhead protection district with twice-annual inspections
Staff to report inspection program results to Plan Commission and Public Works Committee next year
Staff to contact existing salt storage facility near Sternberg about wellhead protection compliance
First comprehensive plan steering committee meeting scheduled for June 1st at 5:00 PM
Staff to send email and calendar invites for steering committee meeting
Electric vehicle charging stations to be added to future agenda for fall discussion
Staff to share Ehlers presentation on residential TIF district qualifications with commission

title: Health & Human Services Committee Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Health & Human Services Committee held an educational orientation meeting for new board members, featuring presentations from the Health Department, Social Services, Veteran Service Office, and Aging Disability Resource Center. No action items were voted on aside from approving previous meeting minutes. The meeting focused on familiarizing committee members with department structures, budgets, and services.

agenda.item: Call to order and remote attendance confirmation
Approval of April 1st committee meeting minutes
Operational functions required by statute (no items)
Educational presentations overview and standing committee ordinance review
Health Department presentation by Caitlyn Suginaka
Social Services Department overview by Administrator Leonard
Veteran Service Office presentation by Aaron Galindo
Aging Disability Resource Center presentation by Mike Ray

discussions.body: Supervisor Cavelli made a motion to approve the minutes. Vice Chair Rosenberg seconded. The motion carried unanimously with all in favor.

Health Officer Caitlyn Suginaka presented an overview of the department's mission and services, including environmental health licensing (over 1,200 licenses), communicable disease prevention, family health programs like Nurse Family Partnership, and the new homeless system response position. She highlighted public health vending machines stocked with free resources like tick kits. The department has approximately 37.6 FTE with about half of its $5 million budget funded by tax levy and the rest through grants and fees.

Administrator Leonard presented on behalf of Director Christa who was unavailable. The department has nearly 140 FTE with a 2026 budget of over $22 million, of which about $7 million is tax levy. He highlighted the Family Keys housing stability program, a successful state pilot addressing housing instability for families in child welfare and youth justice. He noted potential future requests for restructuring within the department due to increased service needs.

Veteran Service Officer Aaron Galindo reported his office increased to 3.0 FTE in 2025 and had 8,149 client interactions, up from 5,900 the previous year. He noted $44 million annually flows to Marathon County veterans, with $2.6 million in retroactive payments in 2025. Key initiatives include surviving spouse benefits outreach, housing stability partnerships with Center for Veterans Issues, and suicide prevention efforts (7 of 30 county suicides in 2025 were veterans). Supervisor Kremes asked about regional coordination; Galindo explained good working relationships exist with neighboring counties but state statute requires each county to have its own VSO.

Executive Director Mike Ray explained the ADRC serves four counties (Marathon, Lincoln, Langlade, Wood) with about 60 FTE and 385-400 volunteers. Marathon County's contribution is $413,135. He described services including information and assistance, caregiver support, dementia care, falls prevention, and Meals on Wheels. Ray noted significant financial challenges as nutrition program costs increased 40-45% over four years while federal funding remained flat, forcing meal and staffing reductions.

actionItems: April 1st committee meeting minutes approved
North Central Healthcare to be scheduled for a separate full presentation before the committee
ADRC Executive Director to present annual report with 2025 data to full board
Next meeting scheduled for Tuesday, June 3rd at 3:00 PM (corrected from originally stated May 29th)
Committee members invited to email Chair Boots and Administrator Leonard with future agenda item suggestions

title: Board of Trustees
overview: The Village of Weston Board of Trustees approved increased park and recreation impact fees for new residential development, passed multiple ordinances including mobile food vendor licensing and e-bike regulations, and held extensive discussion about farmers market location challenges at Kennedy Park. The board also adjourned the Board of Review to June 15, 2026.

agenda.item: Board of Review called to order and adjourned to June 15, 2026
Board of Trustees meeting called to order
Public hearing on park and recreation impact fees (Ordinance 26-010 and Resolution 2026-015)
Acknowledge reports from boards, committees, and commissions with chair updates
Consent agenda - vouchers and license renewals
Ordinances including mobile food vendor, nuisances, e-bikes, and transportation funding resolution
Committee appointments and new business items
Discussion on farmers market location at Kennedy Park

discussions.item: Public Hearing - Park and Recreation Impact Fees
discussions.body: Staff presented a proposal to increase park and recreation impact fees from $300 per single family unit to $761, from $250 to $563 for duplexes, and from $250 to $495 for multifamily units, based on the 2022 public facilities needs assessment. Jim Pitel spoke in public comment questioning whether the village has real data on park maintenance costs. Trustee Motti supported the fees as justified given population growth. Ordinance 26-010 was approved with motion by Lopsorrel, second by Zagami, passed unanimously. Resolution 2026-015 was approved with motion by Hermling, second by Clark, passed unanimously.

Chief Finkey explained this ordinance establishes reciprocal fire inspection agreements between Safer and municipalities including Marshfield, Wisconsin Rapids, Stevens Point, and others. A food truck inspected by any participating department receives a sticker recognized by all, eliminating redundant inspections. Motion by Lopsorrel, second by Zagami, passed unanimously.

The board debated amendments to the remote meeting attendance policy. Original 2023 policy language requiring health, safety, or welfare justifications was contested. Trustee Beck moved to amend by striking sentences requiring specific justifications, keeping only the requirement to notify 12 hours in advance, be on camera, and not participate in closed session. Amendment passed with Trustees Hermling and Motti voting no. Final amended policy passed with Hermling and Motti again voting no.

The president appointed Stephanie Daniels (currently on Finance and HR) and Golden Mua to the CLIPS committee. Motion by Hermling, second by Lopsorrel, passed unanimously. Parks, Finance/HR still have openings, and one more CLIPS opening remains. Board agreed to include committee applications in future packets.

Scoffield Coin and Hobby LLC at 5703 Jamar Street requested secondhand article and jewelry dealer licenses. Chief Hunt explained these dealers must report items to a police-accessible database to prevent fencing of stolen goods. The business location is still under construction, so the board amended the motion to approve contingent on satisfactory background check completion and receipt of occupancy permit. Passed unanimously.

Extensive discussion on challenges at the new Kennedy Park location after losing the Camp Phillips/Scoffield site to development. Concerns raised about parking on grass along Alderson, safety issues, and field damage. Renee explained the market grew from 25 stalls at the original location to 53 at Kennedy Park, and vendors now have their vehicles. Mock Miller Park was discussed but deemed too remote. The board acknowledged this may require ongoing adjustments. Renee confirmed the farmers market operates at a profit through vendor fees, grants, and reimbursements.

Public Works Director Michael Dolan reported the engineering technician position has been advertised multiple times but the specialized skill set is difficult to fill locally. Consulting firms are also struggling to find qualified candidates in central Wisconsin. Two contracts for outside engineering inspection services were approved for projects starting in June.

publicComment: Jim Pitel of 52 Street spoke during public comments criticizing the hiring of outside engineering consultants at $108,000 when the engineering technician position remains unfilled, questioned why Lisa Beck was introduced at a Safer meeting before her official appointment, and complained about lack of Kennedy Park information in the packet. He also spoke during the park impact fee public hearing questioning whether the village has actual data on park maintenance costs.
actionItems: Park and recreation impact fees increased effective with Ordinance 26-010 and Resolution 2026-015
Board of Review adjourned to June 15, 2026
Mobile food vendor reciprocal inspection ordinance adopted
E-bike and nuisance ordinances amended
Remote meeting attendance policy amended to remove specific justification requirements
Stephanie Daniels and Golden Mua appointed to CLIPS committee
Scoffield Coin and Hobby LLC license approved contingent on background check and occupancy permit
Comprehensive plan steering committee meeting scheduled for Monday, June 1st at 6:00 PM
Weston Avenue speed limit study to be completed and brought back to board
Staff to prepare intergovernmental agreement for building inspection assistance to Town of Ringle
Committee applications to be included in future board packets
Continue monitoring farmers market location at Kennedy Park

title: Regular Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Board of Supervisors held an adjourned organizational meeting where they approved committee appointments, elected Supervisors Hapa and Arstead to the Executive Committee, and unanimously approved a resolution reducing landfill tipping fees for disaster-related debris following severe tornado and flood damage in the Ringle area. The board suspended its rules to consider the tipping fee resolution directly from the Solid Waste Management Board due to the emergency nature of the situation.

agenda.item: Pledge of Allegiance and moment of reflection
Reading of meeting notice and roll call
Committee appointments by County Board Chair
Election of at-large Executive Committee members
Ballot counting and announcement of Executive Committee election results
Resolution 24-26: Out of home placement reserve increase
Resolution 26-26: Modification to landfill tipping fees

discussions.body: Chair Gibbs presented committee appointments developed with Vice Chair Boots, emphasizing goals of increasing diversity of perspectives, developing leadership opportunities for newer supervisors (34% in first or second term), and honoring supervisor preferences. Motion by Supervisor Cavelli, seconded by Supervisor Crims. The motion carried but was not unanimous. Supervisor Kratzke noted the system marked him absent but he wanted to be recorded as voting aye.

discussions.item: Election of At-Large Executive Committee Members
discussions.body: Three supervisors were nominated: Supervisor Hapa (nominated by Supervisor Mask), Supervisor Leur (nominated by Supervisor Rosenberg, who praised her advocacy for 'smallest children and oldest elders'), and Supervisor Arstead (nominated by Supervisor Boots). Paper ballots were distributed for supervisors to select two. Supervisors Hapa and Arstead were elected to the Executive Committee.
Items C10 through C14A were approved on motion by Supervisor Cavelli, seconded by Supervisor Drebeck. The motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Resolution 24-26: Out of Home Placement Reserve Increase
discussions.body: Motion by Supervisor Morash, seconded by Supervisor Robinson. The motion carried but was not unanimous.
Motion by Supervisor P, seconded by Supervisor Robinson. The motion carried but was not unanimous.

discussions.item: Resolution 26-26: Modification to Landfill Tipping Fees
discussions.body: Administrator Leonard and Solid Waste Director Hagen Busher presented the proposal to reduce tipping fees for disaster-related debris following severe weather that damaged approximately 44 homes in Ringle. The standard tipping fee of $65/ton would be reduced to $36.94/ton after the state waives $13.10 and Ringle waives $2.33, with the county reducing approximately $13 to cover costs only. Leonard noted the county won't meet the $670,743 threshold for state disaster aid but expects Ringle will. Supervisor Rosenberg requested and received permission for public comment. Residents Jessica Toasty and Matt Haybeck spoke, asking the county to fully waive fees and extend the timeline beyond 60 days. Multiple supervisors asked clarifying questions about fee calculations and coverage. The board first voted unanimously to suspend rules to consider the resolution directly from the Solid Waste Management Board, then approved the resolution unanimously on motion by Supervisor Arstead, seconded by Supervisor Morash.

publicComment: Two residents were granted special permission to speak on the landfill tipping fee resolution. Jessica Toasty emphasized that the state, DNR, and Ringle are waiving fees and asked Marathon County to do the same, noting private businesses have donated up to $40,000 in cleanup assistance. She requested a six-month extension. Matt Haybeck spoke on behalf of homeowners whose houses need demolition, noting many are still waiting for insurance adjusters and need more time for fee relief.
actionItems: Committee appointments approved for the term
Supervisors Hapa and Arstead elected to Executive Committee as at-large members
Resolution 24-26 approved increasing out of home placement reserve
Resolution 25-26 approved amending social services budget
Resolution 26-26 approved reducing landfill tipping fees to $36.94/ton for disaster-related debris through June 17, 2025
Administrator Leonard to continue discussions with Community Foundation about reimbursing uninsured debris disposal costs
Solid Waste Management Board to develop long-term policy on handling disaster-related waste within six months
Staff to notify Supervisor Gao of future Solid Waste Board meetings

title: Environmental Resources Committee Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Environmental Resources Committee approved two rezoning petitions unanimously and voted to forward a modified fee waiver resolution to the county board that will only cover septic system reconnect fees (not other zoning or pouts fees) for properties damaged by the April 17, 2026 tornado. The committee also received educational presentations from Solid Waste, Parks Recreation and Forestry, and Conservation Planning and Zoning departments.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Public hearing - Tim Veland rezoning petition (urban residential/general agriculture to rural residential, Town of Green Valley)
Public hearing - Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning petition (Conservancy Recreation to General Agriculture, Town of McMillan)
Consideration of fee waiver for properties impacted by April 17, 2026 severe weather
Educational presentation - Solid Waste Department overview
Educational presentation - Parks, Recreation and Forestry Department overview
Educational presentation - Conservation Planning and Zoning Department overview

discussions.body: Supervisor Sefeld made a motion to approve the minutes, seconded by Supervisor Drebeck. The motion carried unanimously with no discussion.

discussions.item: Tim Veland rezoning petition - Town of Green Valley
discussions.body: Garrett Pogle presented the staff report for rezoning from urban residential and general agriculture to rural residential for a land division. The Town of Green Valley recommended approval on March 10, 2026. No public comment was offered. Supervisor Drebeck moved to approve based on findings of fact, conclusions of law, and town approval, seconded by Supervisor Kurt. Motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning petition - Town of McMillan
discussions.body: Garrett Pogle presented the petition to rezone from Conservancy Recreation to General Agriculture. Supervisor Leur asked about the conservancy nature of the property; Pogle explained the property contains a home and horse riding stable, and rezoning would make the existing structures conforming uses rather than non-conforming. The Town of McMillan recommended approval on April 20, 2026. No public comment was offered. Supervisor Sefeld moved to approve, seconded by Supervisor Drew. Motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Fee waiver for severe weather impacted properties
discussions.body: Director Lori Mskimmons presented a proposal to waive county zoning and pouts fees for properties damaged by the April 17, 2026 tornado affecting the Town of Ringle and surrounding areas. Significant debate ensued. Supervisor Drew questioned where to draw the line for future events. Supervisor Drebeck expressed concerns about fairness to individuals hit by isolated severe weather who wouldn't qualify. Supervisor Sefeld questioned potential levy impacts. After discussion, Supervisor Sefeld moved to approve the full resolution; Supervisor Leur seconded. Supervisor Drebeck then offered an amendment to limit the waiver to only reconnect fees for pouts systems ($160 each), not other zoning or pouts fees. Supervisor Kurt seconded the amendment. The amendment passed. The amended motion to forward the limited fee waiver to the county board then passed, though not unanimously.

actionItems: Forward rezoning petition for James Offer and David Tract (Town of Green Valley) to Marathon County Board of Supervisors for approval
Forward rezoning petition for Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz (Town of McMillan) to Marathon County Board of Supervisors for approval
Forward amended fee waiver resolution to county board - waiving only pouts reconnect fees ($160) for properties damaged by April 17, 2026 severe weather, not other zoning or pouts fees
Town of Green Valley and Town of McMillan should update comprehensive plans to reflect zoning changes if applicable
CPZ to track costs and requests related to fee waiver program for future policy development

title: Parks Committee
overview: The Village of Weston Parks and Recreation Committee deferred discussion on allowing dogs at the aquatic center to next month, approved installation of new kayak launch signs for the Plover River Water Trail, and received updates on Kennedy Park fundraising and Yellow Banks disc golf course improvements. Public commenters expressed opposition to dogs at the pool and criticized meeting timing and Kennedy Park financial transparency.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Yellow Banks disc golf course update
Dog Day at the aquatic center discussion
Graffiti on the Oaklair River Trail discussion

discussions.body: Minutes from the April 27, 2026 meeting were approved with a motion and second. The motion passed unanimously with no discussion.

Director Sean Oster provided a timeline of the Kennedy Park master plan development from 2023 to present, including the recent May 9th ribbon cutting for Ray Jack and Western Fields. Staff clarified that fundraising by Friends of Kennedy Park has no fixed timeline but the agreement references a 10-year hope for project completion. The Friends group plans to announce fundraising totals at their June tournament with a check presentation to the community foundation. Current focus is raising $117,000 for field lights, with approximately $84-85,000 already at the community foundation. Scott asked about village capital dollars for Kennedy Park; staff confirmed no village funds are currently earmarked pending fundraising progress. The committee chair emphasized giving the volunteer Friends group grace and noted they are not withholding information.

discussions.item: Yellow Banks disc golf course update
discussions.body: Staff presented photos and maps showing tree removal locations along the river and installation of wooden mats over gas pipelines for equipment access. Dan provided documentation of the mat installation process and showed how fallen trees blocked river passage. The committee discussed ongoing coordination with TC Energy (formerly ATC/ANR Pipeline) regarding pipeline protection during construction work.

discussions.item: Dog Day at the aquatic center
discussions.body: The item was deferred to next month because Committee Chair Katrina was absent due to an unforeseen issue. The chair noted the topic was originally brought forward by Katrina and she should have opportunity to speak on it. A public commenter named Andy from 6504 [address unclear] spoke against allowing dogs at the pool, stating 'There should not be animals allowed into a public pool. Period.'

discussions.item: Graffiti on the Oaklair River Trail
discussions.body: Staff explained challenges with graffiti removal including limited equipment access (pressure washer cannot reach the area), heavy concrete benches (2,000 lbs each), and various paint types. Staff has tried multiple graffiti removers with limited success. Committee discussed anti-vandalism coatings and seal coatings as potential solutions. The chair suggested exploring volunteer work days similar to how benches were originally installed through a director team-building day. No formal action was taken; staff will investigate cleaning options.

Dan from Marathon County (2625 Northwestern Avenue) presented version 7 of proposed Plover River Water Trail signs for kayak launches. Signs will be installed at launches 3 (Highway J), 4 (Ross Avenue), and 5 (Yellow Banks) within Weston, with similar signs in Schofield. Signs include QR codes for river gauge levels and donation URL for Marathon County Parks Foundation which funded the signs through state grants. Dan noted Highway J launch may be closed all season due to bridge replacement. Motion to approve village installation of signs passed unanimously.

Staff requested permission to investigate food allergy awareness signage for playgrounds after receiving an email request from a community member. The signs would remind people to keep food at picnic tables rather than on playground equipment. Committee agreed to bring the topic back for further discussion. Staff also announced the Mock Miller Park master plan process will begin with a kickoff meeting with Rettler planned for the following Tuesday.

publicComment: Two public comments were offered. Andy (6504 [street inaudible]) spoke in person opposing dogs at the aquatic center, stating animals should not be allowed in public pools and belong in rivers and lakes. Jim Pensan (5002 [street] Street, Weston) spoke via Zoom expressing dissatisfaction with the 4 p.m. meeting time, stating no public meeting should be held before 5 p.m. He also questioned Kennedy Park funding transparency, calling the $8,700 matching receipts and expenditures 'too coincidental' and asking about the $10,000 grant and Glow event proceeds, characterizing the financial reporting as 'convoluted and misleading.'
actionItems: Minutes from April 27, 2026 approved unanimously
Dog Day at aquatic center discussion deferred to June meeting
Staff to investigate graffiti cleaning solutions and anti-vandalism coatings for Oaklair River Trail
Kayak launch signs approved for installation at Yellow Banks, Ross Avenue, and Highway J launches
Staff to bring food allergy awareness playground signage topic to future meeting
Mock Miller Park master plan kickoff meeting scheduled with Rettler
Bike and pedestrian plan open house scheduled for June 11, 5-7 p.m. in training room
Next meeting scheduled for June 22, 2026

title: Finance and Human Resources Committee
overview: The Village of Weston Finance and Human Resources Committee approved previous meeting minutes and acknowledged April financial reports. The meeting featured an extensive educational presentation from Municipal Clerk Pam on the clerk's office operations, elections, licensing, and budget. Committee member Evan announced his resignation as he is moving out of the village.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Approval of minutes from previous Finance and HR meeting
TIFF one and TIFF two detail reports for April
Educational presentation - Municipal Clerk's office budget and operations
Old business, new business, staff remarks

discussions.item: Approval of minutes from previous Finance and HR meeting
discussions.body: Motion to approve made by Ernling, seconded by Daniels. Motion carried with no discussion.

discussions.item: TIFF one and TIFF two detail reports
discussions.body: Jessica provided an update on TIF district bond payoff timeline. She stated they are anticipating October/November for final payoff, with bonds due October 1st. The CDA will need to pass a resolution to call the bonds, expected this summer. A committee member asked if final numbers still look good, and Jessica confirmed they will be able to pay off the debt.

discussions.item: Acknowledgment of work product transmittals (items 5, 6, and 7)
discussions.body: Motion to acknowledge the April budget status report, TIF detail reports, and legal details report made by Daniels, seconded by Soaper. Motion carried.

Clerk Pam presented extensively on her department's operations with three staff members (two full-time, one part-time). Key topics included: election administration costs varying between even and odd years, Badger Book electronic poll books costing about $11,000 for four units, postage increasing from $6,000 to $15,000 due to fall elections, and pet licensing brought back in-house after 12 years with Pet Data saving approximately $5,495 annually. Discussion occurred about the dog license fee increase from $10 to $20, which Pam explained was due to Marathon County raising fees to cover their $100,000 annual payment to the Humane Society. The quarterly newsletter costs approximately $30,000 annually for postage and printing, with committee members discussing potential cost-saving alternatives. Pam noted that 84% of tax collection ($22 million) is processed through the clerk's office walk-in counter.

Staff member Renee provided an update on the farmers market. She reported attending the last three Saturdays for 6-7 hours each, with the market going well despite some Tuesday wind issues. The market has expanded to include coffee vendors, new food trucks, and craft vendors, with produce vendors expected mid to late June. A committee member who spoke with vendors reported they were very happy with the setup and location. Discussion occurred about parking concerns on Alderson and Newport streets, with staff clarifying that parking is only allowed on the west side of Alderson while Newport has never had parking restrictions.

Committee member Evan announced he would be stepping down from the committee as he and his wife are moving away from Weston, though remaining in the metro area. He thanked the committee for his time serving. The chair noted there are now open spots on this committee and others, encouraging outreach to interested citizens.

actionItems: Minutes from previous Finance and HR meeting approved
April budget status report, TIF detail reports, and legal details report acknowledged
CDA resolution needed this summer to call TIF bonds for October payoff
Recruit new committee members to fill open spots including Evan's vacancy
Next meeting scheduled for Monday, June 15th at 4:30

title: Community Life and Public Safety
overview: The Village of Weston Community Life and Public Safety Committee recommended approval of nuisance code amendments and an e-bike ordinance (with emoto restrictions added), discussed overnight parking regulations without taking action, and authorized demolition proceedings for an abandoned structure. The committee also approved fire inspection requirements for mobile food vendors and addressed several property addressing corrections.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Approval of minutes from previous meeting
Department reports - Code Enforcement, Fire/EMS, Police
E-bike ordinance in Chapter 82 Traffic and Vehicles
Overnight parking regulations October 31 to May 1
Mobile food vendors fire inspection requirements

discussions.body: Jim Senalt spoke regarding concerns about the new farmers market location at the park, stating his opinion that it was 'a terrible execution' and predicting the grassy areas would be destroyed. He raised concerns about ADA accessibility for handicapped parking spaces on grass versus asphalt, questioned the origin of new fire inspection requirements for food trucks, and criticized the accountability timeline for long-standing code violations.

Code Enforcement Officer Audrey reported that 3020 Mountain View Avenue will be removed from the code enforcement roll after property sale and new owner submitted repair permits. Chief Hunt reported the police department had 2,394 calls in March (77 per day), 410 traffic stops, and 281 citations issued. The department has hired its 40th officer, with the 38th starting last week, 39th in May, and 40th around January. Two SROs are assigned to zoning violations for Weston, Rothschild, and Schofield during summer. Motion to acknowledge reports passed unanimously.

The committee reviewed final amendments including corrections to tree limb height requirements (14 and 10 feet switched) and language requiring compliance with jurisdiction authority. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Compie, to recommend approval to the Board of Trustees passed unanimously.

Extensive discussion focused on emotos (electric mini-motorcycles). Chief Hunt stated emotos should not be allowed on roadways, sidewalks, or trails, comparing them to unlicensed dirt bikes that pose safety hazards. The committee discussed whether electric scooters would also be prohibited and decided to leave officer discretion for kids' play scooters. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Chwanka, to recommend approval with amendment prohibiting emotos on roadways, sidewalks, trails, and public property passed unanimously.

Trustee Tolson presented research showing Weston's 12-hour parking restriction (4am-4pm) is significantly longer than surrounding municipalities (most have 4-5 hour windows). Public Works Director Michael indicated the restriction could potentially be eliminated entirely with targeted signage for 6-8 problem areas. Chief Hunt preferred maintaining overnight restrictions for enforcement purposes. No action was taken; Michael will develop a proposal identifying specific exception areas for the July meeting.

Mount Olive Church requested a crosswalk between the church and DC Everest High School parking lot where parishioners park during services. Staff estimated basic crosswalk cost at approximately $2,000. Committee members expressed reluctance to pay for it, noting no accidents have occurred. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Chwanka, to recommend approval contingent on Mount Olive covering installation costs passed unanimously.

Fire Chief explained a regional consortium (Wisconsin Rapids, Marshfield, Stevens Point, Plover, Rib Mountain, and potentially Weston) would share food truck fire inspections with reciprocal recognition via stickers. Wausau opted out to do their own inspections. Fire Marshall Marty already inspected one truck that failed due to improper LP piping and unsecured propane tanks. Motion by Chwanka, seconded by Compie, to approve Ordinance 26-8 passed unanimously.

Staff presented three addressing errors requiring correction for E911 compliance: 5915 Delicoski Street (duplex needing two addresses), Ross Avenue properties out of sequence near an apartment building, and 2211 Radkey Avenue near a new duplex development. Chief Hunt mentioned an additional issue at Weston Villa Apartments on Delicoski with eight units having separate addresses. Motion passed unanimously to recommend approval to the Board of Trustees.

Staff requested authorization to begin raze proceedings for an abandoned structure posted since 2021, abandoned since approximately 2010-2014. A planned fire training burn was cancelled because the structure was too deteriorated for safe training. Current owners are up to date on taxes and preferred this approach; costs will be added to the tax roll. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Compie, passed unanimously.

publicComment: Jim Senalt spoke expressing concerns about the new farmers market location, predicting damage to park grass, questioning ADA compliance for handicapped parking on grass, questioning the need for food truck fire inspections, and criticizing lack of accountability on long-standing code violations.
actionItems: Chapter 50 Nuisance amendments recommended for Board approval
E-bike ordinance with emoto prohibition recommended for Board approval
Michael (Public Works) to develop specific overnight parking exception areas for July meeting
Michael to contact Mount Olive Church regarding crosswalk cost-sharing; if they agree to pay, recommend Board approval
Ordinance 26-8 for mobile food vendor fire inspections approved
Address corrections for three properties recommended for Board approval
Staff authorized to begin raze proceedings for 4104 Shore Avenue
Staff to investigate furniture/dumpster code issue at Sternberg and South Timber
Next meeting scheduled for Monday, July 7, 2025 at 5:00 PM

title: Committee of the Whole Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Wausau School District Board of Education's Committee of the Whole meeting on May 18, 2026 was scheduled to address multiple action items including the preliminary 2026-27 budget, employee handbook updates, meal price changes, and policy revisions. The board was also expected to consider approval of the Riverview HVAC and roofing project and updates to the activities and athletics code.

agenda.item: Excellence in Action: Thomas Jefferson Elementary
Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling (Action Requested)
Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code (Action Requested)

discussions.item: Excellence in Action: Thomas Jefferson Elementary
discussions.body: The board was scheduled to recognize Thomas Jefferson Elementary as part of the Excellence in Action program. No additional details or presenter information were provided in the agenda.

Tabatha Gundrum was scheduled to present the Employee Handbook updates for the 2026-27 school year in an estimated 5 minutes. The board was expected to review and consider approval of the updated handbook.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was scheduled to present the preliminary budget for 2026-27 in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider the initial budget recommendation for the upcoming school year.
Karen Fochs was scheduled to present proposed changes to the 2026-2027 paid meal prices in an estimated 5 minutes. Supporting documentation included a meal price action sheet and PLE tool.

Dr. Jon Tomski and BJ Brandt were scheduled to present suggested changes to the Athletic and Activities Code in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of the 2026-2027 Code of Conduct updates.

discussions.item: Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling
discussions.body: Dr. Jon Tomski was scheduled to present a proposed change to Policy 9270 regarding Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling in an estimated 5 minutes. Action was requested for this policy update.

Dr. Jon Tomski was scheduled to present proposed changes to Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code in an estimated 5 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of these policy revisions.

Ryan Urmanski, Director of Buildings and Grounds, was scheduled to present the Riverview HVAC and roofing project in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of the RFP for this facility improvement project.

publicComment: A public and student comment period was included on the agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to vote on Employee Handbook Updates for 2026-27
Board was expected to vote on the Preliminary 2026-27 Budget recommendation
Board was expected to vote on 2026-2027 Paid Meal Price Update
Board was expected to vote on Activities & Athletics Code changes
Board was expected to vote on Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling revisions

Board was expected to vote on Riverview RFP Approval for HVAC and roofing project

title: Special Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, this Special Meeting of the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to address potential litigation in closed session under Wisconsin State Statute 19.85(g). The board was expected to hold preliminary discussions regarding a legal matter before potentially reconvening in open session to take any necessary action.

agenda.item: Request for Closed Session Pursuant to State Statutes
Reconvene in Open Session, to take further action if necessary and appropriate

discussions.body: The board was scheduled to enter closed session to hold preliminary discussions regarding potential litigation, as permitted under Wisconsin State Statute 19.85(g). This statute allows governmental bodies to convene in closed session when considering matters that could result in legal action against or on behalf of the district.
Following the closed session discussion, the board was expected to reconvene in open session to take any further action deemed necessary and appropriate based on the closed session deliberations.

publicComment: No public comment period was included on this agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to potentially take action following the closed session discussion regarding potential litigation

title: Regular Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to address several significant action items at their May 11, 2026 regular meeting, including approval of the 2026-27 budget reconciliation plan, teacher contract renewals, and wage increases for non-teaching staff. The board was also expected to consider renewal of the WAMCS charter school contract for 2026-2031 and enter closed session regarding personnel matters including non-renewal notices and contract evaluations.

agenda.item: Pledge of Allegiance: Jim Bouche, President
Request for Closed Session Pursuant to State Statutes

discussions.body: The board was scheduled to recognize Maine Elementary as part of the district's Excellence in Action program, which typically highlights achievements and initiatives at individual schools.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was expected to present a summary report of all legal counsel expenses incurred during the third quarter of 2025-2026. The report was described as broken down by law firm and by type of legal advice sought, requiring no action as a written report with an estimated 2-minute presentation time.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was scheduled to present the budget reconciliation plan for budgeting purposes, previously introduced at the April Committee of the Whole meeting. The presentation was estimated at 3 minutes with action requested from the board.
Tabatha Gundrum was scheduled to present a list of teacher contracts for approval for the 2026-27 school year. The presentation was estimated at 5 minutes with action requested from the board.

Tabatha Gundrum was expected to present a recommendation for a 2.63% wage adjustment for all employee groups in the District except teachers, in accordance with the budget reconciliation plan. The agenda noted that teacher negotiations would commence soon with a separate action at a later date, with this presentation estimated at 5 minutes.

Elizabeth Channel, WAMCS Head of School, was scheduled to present the 2026-2031 contract renewal for the Wausau Area Montessori Charter School, previously introduced at the April Committee of the Whole meeting. The presentation was estimated at 2 minutes with action requested.

The board was scheduled to enter closed session pursuant to Wisconsin Statutes sections 19.85(1)(c), (e), and (g) to discuss final notice of non-renewal and contract evaluation matters, with plans to reconvene in open session to take further action if necessary.

publicComment: A public and student comment period was included on the agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to vote on approval of the Consent Agenda including appointments, separations, leaves of absence, retirements, meeting minutes, payment of bills, and donations
Action was requested for the 2026-27 Budget Reconciliation Plan
Board was expected to vote on 2026-27 Teacher Contract Approvals
Action was requested for Various Group Wage/Salary Increase of 2.63% for non-teaching staff
Board was expected to vote on WAMCS Charter School Contract Renewal for 2026-2031
Board was expected to potentially take action following closed session regarding non-renewal notices and contract evaluations

title: Water Works Commission Meeting
overview: The Wausau Water Works Commission approved the 2027-2031 Capital Improvement Projects Budget for Drinking Water and Wastewater Utility, along with two sole source agreements for cross connection control services and polymer chemicals. The commission also received updates on lead service line replacements and wastewater projects.

Discussion regarding the Wastewater - Final WPDES Permit Issued with a permit term of five years

discussions.body: The director provided an update on lead service line replacements. This was an informational report with no action required.

The director reported on wastewater projects including the Headworks Screening Project, Cherry Street Lift Station, and Lift Station Assessment with Clark Dietz. This was an informational report with no action required.
The commission approved the 2027 through 2031 Capital Improvement Projects Budget for the Drinking Water and Wastewater Utility. The motion passed.

discussions.body: The commission approved the sole source renewal agreement for HydroCorp Non-Residential Cross Connection Control Program. The motion passed.

discussions.item: Sole Source Request for MCEP9 and K144L-NA Polymers
discussions.body: The commission approved the sole source request for MCEP9 and K144L-NA polymers used to thicken solids in the Belt Filter Presses and Gravity Belt Thickeners. The motion passed.
The commission discussed the final WPDES permit issued with a five-year term. This was a discussion item with no formal action taken.

title: City Council Meeting
overview: The Wausau Common Council approved mayoral appointments to multiple boards and commissions, passed a consent agenda including bike rack request procedures and special assessments for street construction, but saw contentious debate over proposed amendments to council standing rules with multiple failed motions. The council voted 11-0 on consent items while rule suspension votes were divided, with one failing 5-6.

agenda.item: Week of the Young Child Proclamation
Joint Resolution from the Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee and the Infrastructure & Facilities Committee Approving the Bike Rack Request Form
Resolution from the Infrastructure & Facilities Committee Levying Special Assessments for the 2025 Street Construction Projects
Ordinance from the Common Council to Amend Wausau Municipal Code Ch. 2.16, Standing Rules of the Common Council

Confirming Appointments of the Mayor of the City of Wausau to the Historic Preservation Commission, Sustainability, Energy & Environment Committee, Business Improvement District Board, Community Development Authority Board and Ethics Board

discussions.body: The council approved minutes from both the March 24, 2026 and April 14, 2026 regular meetings. The motion passed unanimously 11-0, moved by Sarah Watson and seconded by Tom Neal.

The consent agenda was approved 11-0, moved by Sarah Watson and seconded by Bruce Trueblood. This included the Joint Resolution Approving the Bike Rack Request Form from the Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee and Infrastructure & Facilities Committee, as well as the Resolution Levying Special Assessments for the 2025 Street Construction Projects.

The council voted to suspend Rule 11(A) regarding referral of ordinances, passing 8-3 with Matt Hoenecke, Lou Larson, and Bruce Trueblood voting no. However, the motion to suspend Rule 21 regarding amending of the rules failed 5-6, with Carol Lukens, Michael Martens, Andrew Wiskowski, Kristin Slonski, Sarah Watson, and Lou Larson voting against.

discussions.item: Ordinance to Amend Wausau Municipal Code Ch. 2.16, Standing Rules of the Common Council
discussions.body: This item saw extensive procedural debate with multiple votes. A motion to approve as amended failed, as did a motion to refer back to the Rules Review Committee. An amendment by Alder Watson subject to attorney review passed, as did a subsequent amendment by Alder Tierney. An amendment proposed by Alder Neal failed.

The council approved the Mayor's appointments to the Historic Preservation Commission, Sustainability, Energy & Environment Committee, Business Improvement District Board, Community Development Authority Board, and Ethics Board. The motion passed.

publicComment: Public comment was included on the agenda both before the business meeting (preregistered citizens) and after the business meeting.

Special assessments levied for 2025 Street Construction Projects
